content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
, FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Nellie Ancel 
Senior Deputy City Attorney 
3300 Capitol A venue, Building A 
PO Box 5006 
Fremont, CA 94537-5006 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-020 
Dear Ms. Ancel: 
March 13, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the City of Fremont regarding 
Section 1090, et seq.1 Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public 
Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering 
advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are 
complete and accurate. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Alameda County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1 (c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requester." (See Section 1097.1 (c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
May HNTB bid on an RFP and contract with the City for the Irvington BART Station site 
plan, station area plan and environmental impact report when HNTB previously contracted with the 
City to prepare a Scoping Report related to the same project? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. Because the contractor chosen for the current RFP is not required to rely on HNTB's 
previous Scoping Report, HNTB may bid on the current RFP and the City may contract with 
HNTB. 
1 Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5. All statutory references are to the Government Code, 
unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-020 
Page No. 2 
You are a Senior Deputy Attorney with the City of Fremont. The City is requesting advice 
regarding its ability to contract with HNTB Corporation to provide consulting services for the 
Irvington BART Station site plan, station area plan and environmental impact report. HNTB had, 
under a prior agreement with the City, prepared a scoping report for the Irvington BART station. 
The Irvington BART Station has been proposed since at least 2003 when it was addressed in 
BART's 2003 Supplemental EIR and a 2006 Final EIS. A preliminary site plan for the station was 
completed in 2006. 
The Scoping Agreement was for a maximum of $75,000. The purpose of the Scoping Report 
prepared by HTNB under the Scoping Agreement was two-fold: (1) to determine if changes to the 
2006 site plan were needed in light of "regulatory, policy and physical" changes that had occurred 
since 2006; and (2) to facilitate funding for the development of the Irvington Bart Station through 
the Alameda County Transportation Commission ("Transportation Commission") Measure BB 
program. The Transportation Commission desired project sponsors to determine the scope, 
schedule, estimated cost and funding needs for projects prior to applying for Measure BB funding 
for the full project. To that end HTNB's tasks included: 
• Through its subconsultant, ICF International, reviewing existing environmental 
impact reports and identifying updates that might be necessary for the next phase of 
the station project; 
• Developing possible station site plan alternatives to address seismic engineering 
issues, site utilization and access (arrangement and capacity of certain elements such 
as parking, bus bays, drop off areas, etc.), property acquisitions, new storm water 
requirements, integration of historic resources, and holding a charrette with the City 
and BART to receive feedback on the proposals; 
• Developing an implementation and phasing plan to carry out the station project; and 
• Developing cost estimates for each phase of the station project and detailed funding 
plan. 
As HNTB carried out the scoping tasks it became clear that the $75,000 budget was 
inadequate to allow detailed site plan alternatives to be developed. As a result, the City instructed 
HTNB to estimate the budget required to complete a new site plan. The Scoping Report contains a 
revised project scope, schedule, cost estimate and funding plan to design and construct the complete 
station to meet the Transportation Commission's requirements. It also contains an overview of the 
site plan issues, a cost estimate to develop a new site plan and recommendations for further study in 
development of the station site plan and environmental clearance update. 
The City received HNTB's Scoping Report in June 2016. In December 2016, the City issued 
its RFP for consultant services for the Irvington BART Station site plan, station area plan and 
environmental impact report. The estimated not-to-exceed compensation for this agreement is 
$1,850,000. The City anticipates that HNTB will submit a proposal for the Subsequent Agreement. 
However, the City is unsure whether contracting with HNTB is permissible in light of HNTB's 
prior work for the City on the Irvington BART Station project. 


File No. A-17-020 
Page No. 3 
The RFP scope of work for the Subsequent Agreement is comprehensive and includes: 
• Developing up to three transportation access scenarios to identify needed transportation 
improvements and levels of vehicle and bike parking to be integrated into the Site Plan and 
Station Area Plan 
• Develop a new site plan for the Irvington BART Station to address access (bike, pedestrian, 
bus bays, drop-off and parking), stormwater management, seismic issues, and integration of 
historic resources. 
• Develop Station site plan alternatives and analysis based on the required elements set forth 
above. 
• Develop the Station Area Plan - a land use plan to guide development in the Station area. 
• Prepare updated environmental clearance documentation 
There is some overlap in the Scoping Agreement and the Subsequent Agreement in the Site 
Plan tasks and the Environmental Clearance tasks. However, the consultant in the Subsequent 
Agreement is not required to use or build on the site plan or other information contained in the 
Scoping Report. The Scoping Report is made available as a resource. Essentially, the consultant is 
to start with a clean slate to develop the station site plan and alternatives. In the Scoping 
Agreement, the consultant was to review and identify possible areas of the existing environmental 
clearance documents that would need updating. In the Subsequent Agreement, the consultant is to 
also review existing environmental clearance documents, determine the updates needed as well as 
the appropriate environmental document needed to clear the project and then prepare the actual 
documents. The scope indicates areas in which the existing documents may need to be updated, but 
asks the consultant to assess and recommend any additional or updated studies. 
The City will evaluate all proposals received in response to the RFP for the Subsequent 
Agreement and select a consultant based on the criteria in the RFP. HNTB has indicated it intends 
to submit a proposal. The City would like advice on whether HNTB's performance of the Scoping 
Agreement would be permissible under Section I 090. 
ANALYSIS 
Section l090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section l090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section l090 is intended .. not only to strike at 
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. 
Bailey (1980) l03 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649.) 


File No. A-17-020 
Page No. 4 
We employ the following six-step analysis to determine whether Section 1090 prohibits a 
public entity from entering into a contract. 
Step One: Is HNTB subject to the provisions of Section 1090? 
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." 
Courts have long found that independent contractors that serve in advisory positions that 
have a potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency 
are subject to Section 1090. (See Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 186 
Cal.App.4th 1114, 1124-1125; Schaefer v. Beri11stein (1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 278, 291 ["statutes 
prohibiting personal interests of public officers in public contracts are strictly enforced. [Citation.] 
. . .  [<(] A person merely in an advisory position to a city is affected by the conflicts of interest 
rule"].) "[I]t seems clear that the Legislature in later amending Section 1090 to include ·employees' 
intended to apply the policy of the conflicts of interest law . . .  to independent contractors who 
perform a public function and to require those who serve the public temporarily the same fealty 
expected from permanent officers and employees." (46 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 74 (1965).) 
While the courts have had many opportunities to find that individual consultants are subject 
to Section 1090, a recent opinion has also found that Section 1090 also applies to corporate 
consultants. (Davis v. Fresno Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300.) 
The courts focus the inquiry regarding whether an independent contractor is considered an 
"employee" under Section 1090 on the scope of influence the independent contractor holds in 
advising the public entity. The court in Hub City determined that an independent contractor that 
exerts considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency is subject to Section 
1090. (Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton, supra, 186 Cal.App.4th at pp. 1124-
1125.) Similarly, in Schaefer, the court found that a contractor who was "merely in an advisory 
position" was also subject to Section 1090. (Schaefer v. Berinstein, supra, 140 Cal.App.2d at p. 
291.) 
In the Chadwick Advice Letter, No A-15-147 a contractor was involved in designing a golf 
course project that it then wished to bid on and build. The contractor entered into a contract with the 
city to develop a general plan that would lay out the design of a reconstructed golf course. The 
contractor advised the city, worked closely with city staff and project manager, and ultimately 
designed and developed the plan that later became the RFP. In the Chadwick letter the threshold 
question was: 'Does Section 1090 consider a corporate consultant that advises a public entity on the 
design phase of a project to be an 'employee'? The contractor was in a position to interact with and 
advise the city on its policy goals, create a design that interprets and applies the City's stated plan 
for the golf course, and work closely with the project manager and other staff to ensure the City and 
community supported the design. We found that because the contractor acted in an advisory 
capacity with the capability of exerting influence over the city staffs decision making with respect 
to the RFP, it was subject to Section 1090. 


File No. A-17-020 
Page No. 5 
In this case, HNTB completed an implementation and phasing plan to carry out the 
Irvington BART station project and drafted a Scoping Report that contained a revised project scope, 
a schedule, and a cost estimate and funding plan to design and construct the complete station to 
meet specified requirements. HNTB's Scoping Report also contains an overview of the site plan 
issues, a cost estimate to develop a new site plan and recommendations for further study in 
development of the station site plan and environmental clearance update. Therefore, HNTB was in 
an advisory position to the city and was able to exert considerable influence over the city staffs 
decision-making through its Scoping Report on the Irvington BART Station project. Therefore, 
Section 1090 applies to HNTB as a corporate consultant. 
Step Two: Does the decision involve a contract? 
To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general 
principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234 
( 1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Sections 1090 and 1097 require 
that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 
'contract."' (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 351 citing Stigall, supra, at pp. 569, 571.) We have 
previously found that a request for proposal is a contract under Section 1090 because the public 
entity will contract with the winning bidder. (See Kies Advice Letter, A-14-101.) Additionally, the 
resulting contract between the successful bidder and the City would be subject to Section 1090, 
therefore there is a contract involved in this decision. 
Step Three: Is the official making or participating in making a contract? 
Section 1090 casts a wide net to capture those officials who participate in any way in the 
making of the contract. (People v. Sobel ( 1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052.) Therefore, for purposes 
of Section 1090, participating in making a contract is defined broadly as any act involving 
preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing plans and 
specifications, and solicitations for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae 
(1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237; see also Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) 
Here, we must answer the question as to whether HTNB' s work on the Scoping Report 
constitutes making or participating in the current RFP. Though HTNB created a Scoping Report, 
the consultant being sought in the current RFP is not required to use or build on the site plan or 
other information contained in the Scoping Report. As stated above, the Scoping Report is made 
available as a resource. Also, as noted above, the consultant hired under the new RFP is to start with 
a clean slate to develop the station site plan and alternatives. In the Scoping Agreement, HTNB was 
to review and identify possible areas of the existing environmental clearance documents that would 
need updating. In the Subsequent Agreement, the consultant is to also review existing 
environmental clearance documents, determine the updates needed as well as the appropriate 
environmental document needed to clear the project and then prepare the actual documents. The 
scope indicates areas in which the existing documents may need to be updated, but asks the 
consultant to assess and recommend any additional or updated studies. 
In the Chadwick Letter the contractor had been intricately involved in designing the RFP 
and the RFP was specific on exactly how the project was to be completed. In this case, while there 
is some overlap between the tasks required for the Scoping Report and the RFP, one does not 


File No. A-17-020 
Page No. 6 
necessarily determine the other as was the case in Chadwick. As you have stated, the contractor 
selected under the current RFP may begin with a clean slate and not rely upon the Scoping Report 
Therefore, HNTB has not participated in the RFP for purposes of Section 1090, and may contract 
with the City should it be selected. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
SKB:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Sukhi K. Brar 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
May HNTB bid on an RFP and contract with the City for the Irvington BART Station site 
plan, station area plan and environmental impact report when HNTB previously contracted with the 
City to prepare a Scoping Report related to the same project?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. Because the contractor chosen for the current RFP is not required to rely on HNTB's 
previous Scoping Report, HNTB may bid on the current RFP and the City may contract with 
HNTB. 
1 Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5.

sections.analysis:
Section l090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section l090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section l090 is intended .. not only to strike at 
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. 
Bailey (1980) l03 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649.)  
We employ the following six-step analysis to determine whether Section 1090 prohibits a 
public entity from entering into a contract. 
Step One: Is HNTB subject to the provisions of Section 1090? 
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members."

have a potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency

Cal.App.4th 1114, 1124-1125; Schaefer v. Beri11stein (1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 278, 291 ["statutes 
prohibiting personal interests of public officers in public contracts are strictly enforced. [Citation.] 
. . .  [<(] A person merely in an advisory position to a city is affected by the conflicts of interest 
rule"].) "[I]t seems clear that the Legislature in later amending Section 1090 to include ·employees' 
intended to apply the policy of the conflicts of interest law . . .  to independent contractors who 
perform a public function and to require those who serve the public temporarily the same fealty 
expected from permanent officers and employees." (46 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 74 (1965).) 
While the courts have had many opportunities to find that individual consultants are subject

The courts focus the inquiry regarding whether an independent contractor is considered an 
"employee" under Section 1090 on the scope of influence the independent contractor holds in 
advising the public entity. The court in Hub City determined that an independent contractor that 
exerts considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency is subject to Section 
1090. (Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton, supra, 186 Cal.App.4th at pp. 1124-
1125.) Similarly, in Schaefer, the court found that a contractor who was "merely in an advisory 
position" was also subject to Section 1090. (Schaefer v. Berinstein, supra, 140 Cal.App.2d at p. 
291.) 
In the Chadwick Advice Letter, No A-15-147 a contractor was involved in designing a golf 
course project that it then wished to bid on and build. The contractor entered into a contract with the

question was: 'Does Section 1090 consider a corporate consultant that advises a public entity on the 
design phase of a project to be an 'employee'? The contractor was in a position to interact with and

for the golf course, and work closely with the project manager and other staff to ensure the City and 
community supported the design. We found that because the contractor acted in an advisory 
capacity with the capability of exerting influence over the city staffs decision making with respect 
to the RFP, it was subject to Section 1090.  
In this case, HNTB completed an implementation and phasing plan to carry out the 
Irvington BART station project and drafted a Scoping Report that contained a revised project scope, 
a schedule, and a cost estimate and funding plan to design and construct the complete station to 
meet specified requirements. HNTB's Scoping Report also contains an overview of the site plan 
issues, a cost estimate to develop a new site plan and recommendations for further study in 
development of the station site plan and environmental clearance update. Therefore, HNTB was in 
an advisory position to the city and was able to exert considerable influence over the city staffs 
decision-making through its Scoping Report on the Irvington BART Station project. Therefore,

Step Two: Does the decision involve a contract? 
To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general 
principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234 
( 1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Sections 1090 and 1097 require 
that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 
'contract."' (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 351 citing Stigall, supra, at pp. 569, 571.) We have

Section 1090 casts a wide net to capture those officials who participate in any way in the

selected under the current RFP may begin with a clean slate and not rely upon the Scoping Report 
Therefore, HNTB has not participated in the RFP for purposes of Section 1090, and may contract 
with the City should it be selected.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May HNTB bid on an RFP and contract with the City for the Irvington BART Station site 
plan, station area plan and environmental impact report when HNTB previously contracted with the 
City to prepare a Scoping Report related to the same project?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Because the contractor chosen for the current RFP is not required to rely on HNTB's 
previous Scoping Report, HNTB may bid on the current RFP and the City may contract with 
HNTB. 
1 Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5.

We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Alameda County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. QUESTION May HNTB bid on an RFP and contract with the City for the Irvington BART Station site plan, station area plan and environmental impact report when HNTB previously contracted with the City to prepare a Scoping Report related to the same project? CONCLUSION Yes. Because the contractor chosen for the current RFP is not required to rely on HNTB's previous Scoping Report, HNTB may bid on the current RFP and the City may contract with HNTB. 1 Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. FACTS File No. A-17-020 Page No. 2 You are a Senior Deputy Attorney with the City of Fremont. The City is requesting advice regarding its ability to contract with HNTB Corporation to provide consulting services for the Irvington BART Station site plan, station area plan and environmental impact report. HNTB had, under a prior agreement with the City, prepared a scoping report for the Irvington BART station. The Irvington BART Station has been proposed since at least 2003 when it was addressed in BART's 2003 Supplemental EIR and a 2006 Final EIS. A preliminary site plan for the station was completed in 2006. The Scoping Agreement was for a maximum of $75,000. The purpose of the Scoping Report prepared by HTNB under the Scoping Agreement was two-fold: (1) to determine if changes to the 2006 site plan were needed in light of "regulatory, policy and physical" changes that had occurred since 2006; and (2) to facilitate funding for the development of the Irvington Bart Station through the Alameda County Transportation Commission ("Transportation Commission") Measure BB program.
